title: Bayesian Hierarchical Linear Model
text: Let g = 1,\ldots,K index treatment groups = 1,\ldots,N_{\text{subj}} index subjects. Subject belongs treatment group g_i \\{1,\ldots,K\}. Let V_{} > 0 denote observed tumor volume subject time t_{}, define log-volume y_{} = \log(V_{}). subject measurement n, y_{} \mid \alpha_i, \beta_i, \sigma \sim \mathcal{N}\left(\alpha_i + \beta_i t_{}, \sigma^2\right), \alpha_i subject-specific intercept (baseline log-volume t=0),\beta_i subject-specific slope (log-scale growth rate), \sigma>0 residual standard deviation. Define subject-specific parameter vector \theta_i = \begin{pmatrix} \alpha_i \\ \beta_i \end{pmatrix}, \qquad \mu_g = \begin{pmatrix} \alpha_g \\ \beta_g \end{pmatrix}. Conditional treatment group g_i, \theta_i \mid g_i, \mu_{g_i}, \Sigma \sim \mathcal{N}_2(\mu_{g_i}, \Sigma). parameterize random-effects covariance matrix \Sigma = D R D, \qquad D = \mathrm{diag}(\tau_\alpha,\tau_\beta), \qquad R = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & \rho \\ \rho & 1 \end{pmatrix}, \tau_\alpha>0 \tau_\beta>0 standard deviations random intercept random slope, \rho \(-1,1) correlation. g=1,\ldots,K, \alpha_g \sim \mathcal{N}(0,10^2), \qquad \beta_g \sim \mathcal{N}(0,10^2). Residual standard deviation: \sigma \sim \text{Half-}\mathcal{N}(0,5^2). Random-effects standard deviations: \tau_\alpha \sim \text{Half-}\mathcal{N}(0,5^2), \qquad \tau_\beta \sim \text{Half-}\mathcal{N}(0,5^2). Correlation matrix prior: R \sim \mathrm{LKJ}(2). \begin{aligned} y_{} \mid \alpha_i,\beta_i,\sigma &\sim \mathcal{N}(\alpha_i+\beta_i t_{},\sigma^2),\\ \theta_i \mid g_i &\sim \mathcal{N}_2(\mu_{g_i}, \Sigma),\\ \alpha_g &\sim \mathcal{N}(0,10^2), \quad \beta_g \sim \mathcal{N}(0,10^2),\\ \sigma &\sim \text{Half-}\mathcal{N}(0,5^2),\\ \tau_\alpha,\tau_\beta &\sim \text{Half-}\mathcal{N}(0,5^2), \quad R \sim \mathrm{LKJ}(2). \end{aligned}

text: encounter warning “Model failed converge max|grad|” fitting model lmm(), one common cause poor scaling time variable. simple effective fix rescale time larger unit (example, days months). Using melanoma2 dataset, following code produces convergence warning: case, converting time days months resolves convergence issue. , create new time variable dividing average number days per month, update tumr object, refit model: rescaling time variable, model fits without producing convergence warning. what: Putting lmm() Summary Plot on the Log Scale
title: Troubleshooting
text: viewing summary plot lmm(), may see message: “Model log1p-transformed response. Back-transforming predictions original response scale.Standard errors still transformed scale.” message appears lmm() fits model using log1p-transformed response default, plot() method automatically back-transforms predicted values original response scale. prefer view predicted volume values log scale, can apply log transformation directly plot’s y-axis. Since summary plot ggplot2 object, can done using scale_y_continuous() log1p transformation scales package.  approach preserves model fit allowing visualize predictions uncertainty transformed scale.
code: lmm_mel2 <- lmm(mel2) plot(lmm_mel2, "response") + ggplot2::scale_y_continuous(trans = scales::log1p_trans()) Model has log1p-transformed response. Back-transforming predictions to   original response scale. Standard errors are still on the transformed   scale. Scale for y is already present. Adding another scale for y, which will replace the existing scale.

title: Troubleshooting
text: lmm() function allows override default model formula, providing flexibility fitting alternative model specifications. default, lmm() fits model form log1p(measure) ~ group * time + (time | id) (example, Volume ~ months + (1 | ID)). example , default formula replaced simpler model uses rescaled time variable random intercept : model fits successfully, calling summary() object fail. occurs summary() method designed around default lmm() model structure currently assumes formula. result, alternative formulas may fully supported summary(). using custom formula, keep mind downstream methods—summary()—may behave expected.
code: lmm_mel2 <- lmm(                  tumr_obj = mel2,                  formula = "Volume ~ months + (1 | ID)"                )

title: Getting started with the tumr package
text: introducing tumr functionality, first construct simple plotting function illustrate common pitfall tumor growth visualization. Note: plot_mean() part tumr package. defined solely provide baseline visualization comparison tumr’s methods.
code: plot_mean <- function(data, group, time, measure, id, stat = median, remove_na = FALSE){   data_summary <- data |>     dplyr::group_by({{group}}, {{time}}) |>     dplyr::summarise(measure = stat({{measure}}, na.rm = remove_na), .groups = "drop_last") |>     dplyr::ungroup()    if (remove_na == TRUE) {     data_full <- data |>       na.omit(data)   } else {     data_full <- data   }    ggplot2::ggplot() +     ggplot2::geom_line(data = data_full,                        ggplot2::aes(x = {{time}},                                     y = {{measure}},                                     group = {{id}},                                     color = {{group}}),                        alpha = 0.5)  +     ggplot2::geom_line(data = data_summary,                        ggplot2::aes(x = {{time}},                                     y = measure,                                     color = {{group}}),                        linewidth = 1.2) +     ggplot2::labs(             y = "Volume",             title = "Volume over Time"           )  }

title: Getting started with the tumr package
text: One primary analysis tools tumr rfeat() function, implements response feature analysis. two-stage approach simplifies complex longitudinal data extracting single interpretable summary measure per subject. Specifically, rfeat(): Computes growth slope (beta coefficient) subject Averages slopes within treatment group Compares group-level summaries using one following methods: t-test ANOVA Tukey post-hoc test ANOVA Tukey post-hoc tests example uses comparison = “” perform ANOVA followed Tukey post-hoc comparisons. plot() method rfeat objects displays individual subject slopes group-level means.

title: Getting started with the tumr package
text: tumr package also includes lmm(), fits linear mixed effects model tumor growth data. Linear mixed models well suited longitudinal data account : Fixed effects: population-level effects interest (e.g., treatment, time) Random effects: subject-specific variability induces correlation among repeated measurements default, lmm() fits model: log1p(measure) ~ group * time + (time | id) specification allows subject growth trajectory estimating overall treatment effects. model formula can customized desired. Note: may see convergence warning fitting model. occurs, see Troubleshooting article guidance. summary() method lmm objects uses emmeans package report: overall effect time Treatment-specific slopes time Statistical tests comparing slope differences groups Finally, tumr provides plot() method lmm objects produces two visualizations: Predicted tumor growth trajectories time Estimated mean growth slopes group confidence intervals    Since lmm() model data log scale default straight line corresponds exponential growth log scale, can check whether exponential growth model fits well plotting residuals Linear Mixed Model vs time.

title: Getting started with the tumr package
text: package also includes quad(), fits quadratic linear mixed effects model tumor growth data. model useful tumor growth time may nonlinear rather strictly linear. Quadratic linear mixed models well suited longitudinal tumor growth data account : Fixed effects: population-level effects interest, treatment, time, quadratic effect time Random effects: subject-specific variability induces correlation among repeated measurements default, quad() fits model: \log(1 + \text{measure}) \sim (\text{time} + \text{time}^2) * \text{group} + (\text{time} \mid \text{id})
code: mel2 <- tumr(melanoma2, ID, Day, Volume, Treatment) quad_obj <- quad(mel2) plot(quad_obj)

text: addition linear mixed model, package also supports fitting Bayesian hierarchical linear model. Detailed usage model described article. summary() method provides posterior summaries : Treatment-specific intercepts exponential transformation Treatment-specific slopes exponential transformation Pairwise treatment contrasts slopes exponential transformation Posterior means credible intervals reported quantities. plot() method visualizes posterior summaries, including: Posterior predictive mean trajectories 95% credible intervals Treatment-specific slope estimates 90% credible intervals Pairwise slope contrasts 90% credible intervals MCMC trace plots model diagnostics

what: Analysis and Power Calculations for Tumor Growth Rates
title: Analysis and Power Calculations for Tumor Growth Rates
text: tumr collection tools analyzing tumor growth data. example use tumr can seen Get Started page. tumr includes: tumr() - creates tumr object can passed plot_median(), rfeat(), lmm(). plot_median() - creates spaghetti plot plots median tumor growth measurement treatment group. rfeat() - tests whether beta coefficients (slope tumor measure time) individuals different treatment groups different using either t-test, ANOVA, Tukey, ANOVA Tukey. rfeat() plot() function shows individual beta coefficients mean slope treatment group. lmm() - fits linear mixed model default formula log1p(Volume) ~ Treatment * scale(Day) + (scale(Day) | ID) time variable centered. lmm() includes summary() function provides overall effect time averaged levels treatment group, slope treatment time treatment group, results contrast test slope differences treatment groups. lmm() includes plot() function shows predicted values tumor growth measurements time mean slope treatment 95% confidence intervals. bhm() - fits Bayesian Hierarchical Linear Model. bhm() includes summary() function provides treatment-specific intercepts exponential transformation , treatment-specific slopes exponential transformation, pairwise treatment contrasts slopes exponential transformation. bhm() includes plot() function shows mean slope treatment 90% credible intervals, Pairwise slope contrasts 90% credible intervals, MCMC trace plots model diagnostics.
